top_rant.text:
UPDATED 8/6/2014
I had to dock this one star simply because, now I own 4 Cameos instead of 2 and half of them have had return-worthy issues (the exact same issues). The other two have so many quirks that I know for a fact that I cannot cut certain items on certain machines. Additionally, the memory management of the Studio software is HORRIFIC. By that I mean that running Studio will suck up more computer memory than running both Adobe Illustrator and Adobe Photoshop combined. There is no excuse for that. I have a higher end computer with great memory and a great processor. If you have a medium to low performance computer I do not know how you can even run this software at all. This program runs SO SLOWLY after awhile that recently it literally took me 45 minutes to print one file because it rendered the file much larger than it needed to be (it had a lot of font/ words, NOT graphics) and so it took that long just to spool to my printer after freezing and crashing multiple times. There is a software upgrade (v.3) that should honestly never have been released because it is so buggy I had to uninstall it. Using the upgraded software, the Cameos simply would not cut all the way through the paper on any of my machines no matter how new the blades were that I was using. There are also multiple other issues with the "upgraded" software package. Silhouette America made a huge mistake releasing that software when it did and until there are MAJOR improvements in it, I will not re-install it.

BACKGROUND:
I use the Silhouette Cameo in my small crafting business. I currently own two of them and have a third on the way (its a busy business). I use my Cameo everyday. I would have given the machine 5 stars but, I am currently in the process of returning one machine and making a warranty claim. I simply got a dud of a machine and 9 months and countless support calls later, I am simply going to return it. That being said, my other Cameo is a workhorse but has some limitations.

EASE OF USE
HARDWARE AND INCLUDED ACCESSORIES:
The Cameo is super easy to use once you get the hang of it. Plan to spend the first day or two wasting paper and testing things out. The hardware is straight forward and not too technical to get the hang of. The blade is easy to insert and the lcd screen is easy to understand. What is not so straight forward is using the Cameo with an SD card only. While it is easy to save the image to SD and pop the card into the machine, once you get there, it is very confusing trying to open the image and change the cut settings via the SD screen. But, maybe I need to play with this option a little more.

BLADES:
The cutting blade was a constant source of irritation at first. For some reason, the first 5-6 blades I purchased did not have removable tips. Then I, somehow, managed to get a blade with a removable tip and that made ALL the difference. Little bits of paper can get stuck in the blade housing which can negatively affect the performance of the blade so, trying to fish tiny bits of paper out of an even tinier hole is almost impossible without the removable tip. So, hopefully, the removable tip is here to stay because it is invaluable.

MATS:
The cutting mats are excellent until they're not anymore. I have only had one mat (out of the probably 100 that I have owned) that was insanely sticky out the pack - so much so that it shredded and destroyed the one piece of paper I used on it. All of the other mats I have owned are great for a good while. I have kept some numbers and I can usually get 50-60 full page cuts out of a 12x12 mat before it becomes difficult to use. I have tried all of the popular re-stick methods (spray glue, Re-Nu sheets, Slice mat adhesive, Xyron adhesive etc.) and none of them are ideal. Most leave you with not enough adhesive or adhesive that sticks to the paper, not the mat. I have also tried Cricut mats, because they are cheaper, but the quality is not as good as Silhouette branded mats. Again, I use the Cameo for high volume cutting so, if you are just a hobbyist, one mat should last a long time.

I use the scraper religiously. I do not believe it comes with the Cameo and needs to be purchased separately but it is well worth it.

SOFTWARE:
The included software is ok. It is intuitive to use but has some functionality problems in my opinion. Opening large graphics is my biggest issue. If the graphic is too large, the Studio software will simply stop displaying it or printing it properly, even if you shrink it down. Any document containing the image will not save and will become wildly distorted. Large images need to be shrunk in another software before they are opened in Studio. From what I can gather from support on this issue, this may not be a problem in the designer version of the software, which costs an additional 50.00.

Also, although you can use all of the fonts installed on your PC, I find that a lot of the fonts are limited in that you cannot bold, underline or italicize them for some reason - the options are there, they are just greyed out. Additionally, although I wouldn't say the Studio software crashes frequently, it does crash and has crashed many times. If you have not saved your work, you are out of luck when it decides to crash. I would say that the software freezes often though, especially when sending something to the printer. It will say "Not responding" and spin for awhile but then, eventually, it will unfreeze. If you are cutting something and trying to print something else out, everything will stop for awhile and freeze up. It eventually will come back though.

Finally, I find that if you have many images or even a lot of font on your project page, the Studio software simply cannot keep up, it uses a lot of computer memory and drags noticeably to the point where there is a 5-7 second lag between me telling it to do something and it actually doing it. I have a high performance computer that can run Photoshop, Illustrator and other large apps simultaneously without a problem so, it is not my computer, it is the software.

QUALITY OF CUTS:
I cut perhaps 30-40 pages a day on my Cameo. I use everything from 110# cardstock to super thin (read: crappy) sticker paper. The Cameo can cut a variety of materials well. The thicker cardstock needs to be double cut on a low speed though and, because of this, it can take forever to cut a full sheet of shapes on the thicker stuff. But, cutting stickers is super fast and easy to do.

With more intricate cuts, The Cameo is hit or miss - mostly hit. But, I find I have much more waste than with simpler shapes. If I have 10 intricate shapes on my sheet, I may lose 3 of them to ripping or messy cutting. This is true no matter the cut speed, blade depth or material I am cutting.

PRINT AND CUT:
The Print and Cut feature is amazing normally but does have its drawbacks for sure. The default margins for print and cut are huge so if you use the default settings, you will waste a ton of the page on empty space. But, I find that if you change the default settings, the accuracy of the cut decreases. Additionally, I have found that there is one certain print that the Cameo simply does not like - if you have a printed outline on your shape and you want the Cameo to cut out the shape leaving a little space between the printed outline and the cut line, I have found that the accuracy of the cuts drops dramatically. For instance, if I have 20 shapes on a page, I may lose 15-16 of them because they are being cut off-center. That is an enormous amount of waste and limits the design potential.

SILHOUETTE BRANDED PRODUCTS:
Of the Silhouette branded products, I have purchased and used the white sticker paper, foil paper, chalkboard vinyl, adhesive fabric paper, clear sticker paper, regular vinyl, double sided adhesive, magnet paper, adhesive magnet paper, and tattoo paper. I have found that MOST if not ALL of the silhouette branded materials are wildly overpriced for what you are getting and, at the end of the day. it is not necessary to purchase the Silhouette brand. For example, the printable white sticker paper runs about 1.00 per page and you get 8 pages in a pack. This is nothing but plain white label paper and you can get something identical for a dime per page elsewhere. You may need to slightly tweak the suggested cut settings but I find you need to do that anyway because the suggested settings for many of the Silhouette branded materials do not work well for me. Additionally, I have found that materials such as the foil paper and clear sticker paper DO NOT print well in a laser printer and do not print at all in an inkjet printer. I think they are formulated for laser printing but it does not say that and, in any case, does not print properly. I find that much cheaper and comparable foil and clear paper print WAY better than the Silhouette branded items. I have two laser printers and 1 inkjet and it is the same story in all the printers. Also, the instructions on the packaging is almost non-existent on the branded products and the online videos are really not helpful to me.

CONCLUSION:
The Cameo ultimately is an amazing machine. Is it perfect? No. But, it also is in the 200-300 dollar range which, for a digital cutting machine that does not require expensive cartridges, is a good deal. I would never consider buying a machine that requires you to purchase cartridges separately as you can import and create a great many shapes yourself on the Cameo. Understand the limitations of the machine and it will give you lots of crafting pleasure. Also know that, if your machine does not work and you are fairly certain it is not user error, you may just have a bad machine.

top_rant.text:
Love this kit. Great starter kit for kids n adults alike. I typically use sterling but lost all my stuff n picked it up for a great starter kit again. The box of beads is great also and had a very nice mix n selection of beads also. A few there were only 3 or 4 of and to be expected also. A nice variety with a dozen or so of each n just what I wanted. Packaged nicely, arrived on time and I am very happy with the purchase n would recommend it to anyone asking or interested in a starter kit. There was even a nice little pile of dyed Mother of pearl which are beautiful also and always fun.

One thing I will say to those always complaining about dyed stoned. IT is VERY COMMON and done ALL the time and doesn't make a stone fake. Opsl, opalite, are quartz crystal, unless they state glass, that is dyed n heat treated. Real opal you could NEVER get for such prices n the imitation created from crystal for artists n jewelers wanting something opal like that is not so high. Stones like turquoise n lapis are mined out and the STANDARD is howlite or magnesite which are both stones and healing stones also and used nowadays for creating imitation turquoise etc because both are white with veins n howlite being even more white and they are porous stones which makes them excellent stones for absorbing dye also so they are the most common stones used for imitation stones like turquoise which has been mined out unless you like black. Strawberry, cherry, and pineapple quartz are simply crystal quartz that has been dyed to create some new and interesting stones n colors. It is NOT some kind of fake or fraud but an intentional creation by man to add some new things to the market. Nobody is trying to fool or fake out anybody but is COMMON KNOWLEDGE in the market if your a bead artist or designer that has learned n knows the business. Most stones are dyed nowadays because mankind has raped the earth dry of quality stones n that's what you have to do to get color. Many stones that are abundant like jaspers obsidian pipe stone etc and even quartz crystals are found in abundance and others are only found in one or two places in the whole world. Precious stones are off the charts and white opal n fire opal are the same n very hard to get as opposed to Mexican opal which has purple veins and abundant. Coral is the same way and all but GONE and bamboo coral a real coral is now used, always DYED red, or BLEACHED white to give it its rich coral color. So when people get stones that are dyed n know nothing about stones or the business I keep seeing reviews calling them fake etc when they are NOT. Sometimes opal is done on glass and when it is they state it. If it's not stated at all it means it is the STANDARD and done using quartz crystal. Same with the others. When it is fake it is "synthetic" stone meaning manmade and usually some forn of plastic or acrylic is used or they will state acrylic or even glass. If nothing is stated and it says "imitation" that means magnesite or howlite is used, or in the case of clear stones it is quartz crystal. The 3 I named they don't even state anything because people in the market already know what they are and that they are quartz crystal that is dyed to create some new colors for artists. Also chips are judt that, chips. They are not rocks or big nuggets but chips. Chips are the SCRAPS that come from cutting out the grade A-B portions of any rock or crystals leaving the C-D grade chips/scraps which are then tumbled n drilled into stones very irregular in size n nature n the holes are NEVER centered but a rare on here n there because it's impossible to do. If you want chip like stones more even and consistent in size with holes centered then look for "pebbles" NOT CHIPS. If you want larger stones than chips thsn look for "nuggets" NOT CHIPS. Chips are just what they are called, small chips of stone that are made from the chips left over from cutting stones of high quality to prevent waste of thr precious stones which are getting harder n harder to find or get in A-B grades n those grades are used for the more expensive beads n cabochons. The market standard n grading n rating or describing of stones doesn't have to change to meet the standard of ignorance amongst humans it is humans that need to learn how the market works n defines things and what is what before they purchase just like any ither business. Check mohs hardness because those low on the scale often break if your not careful etc, read the descriptions n look at what 3mm or 5mm is in a bead chart because pictures are usually close up so you can see the product not the size because chips ARE small n the actusl size is in the description n chips rarely come larger than 8mm most are more like 3-6mm. They are chips. Especially gem stone chips. Rock chips found in abundance are usually larger good color consistent color n very little breakage. Gemstone n crystsls smaller more fragile n a whole range of color with very little A grade color and often little B grade color and most stones that do have good color it's is because they are dyed to give them better color. No fake, no hoax by anyone, stones are often heat treated dyed, bleached etc and is common knowledge in the market as well as all the reasons why treatment is done. So people need to learn about gem stones n rocks n how the market works before purchasing n read the descriptions instead of lookin at the pictures. KNOW what your doing n what is what n how marketers label things as well as hoe stones are treated, which ones, n why and then you won't be disappointed. If you want a bunch of specific stones then buy those specific stones n don't buy a mix because mixes are a crap shoot n mix of random stones. Your gonna get mire of some and less of others maybe even only one. They are a mix and often the left overs from stranding chips or strands that in which the string breaks so they throw them in a mix n just sell them random mix because artists n designers like them n know what they are getting n mean when they say mix, or chip, or imitation, synthetic, or not labeled at all even. Anyone that knows turquoise can look at snd tell if it's real because you never get a good strand of turquoise that is all uniform in rich color. Natural has a lot of black and in dmsl batches can be all black. The REAL good stuff is GONE but in very small amounts n cost a lot of money to get. Natural is more black than turquoise in color at all. So do your homework, learn about stones n what is rare, or almost gone n what is abundant and understand that dyed does NOT mean fake. The market doesn't have to change how it does things for you, you need to learn how the market works n what's what before you buy. Just like cars or anything and chips are chips NOT nuggets or rocks but chips from them. Someone asks you if you want to buy a diamond chip dor $10 do you honestly think they are going to sell you a half carat or carat diamond? No. A CHIP from one. So to with any semi-precious gem stones n crystals only a bit larger than precious gems and rocks even more. Gems n semi precious gems are also very fragile n break easy especially the smaller thinner they are etc. So expect it because it happens all the time. Rocks much harder dont. Learn what your working with so you know n read the descriptions. I keep seen complaints when it's written right IN the descriptions n even people asking questions that are in the descriptions. Read them FIRST BEFORE you buy n learn what mm are n look up the size so you know what it looks like. Smaller than I thought or expected when it says right IN it they are that small then complain is just wrong. Never use the pictures ALWAYS go by the descriptions n ask questions after you read them and before you purchase if you have questions. People look at pictures of cars online all the time yet when you order one you don't write bad reviews cuz it's bigger than the picture. Shoes anything. Pictures are deceptive n beads b chips are very small things so often they do close up for better viewing n even tell you in the descriptions colors vary due to the nature n wide range of colors on stones. Believe it. Don't expect grade A stones in chips because no fool smashes up grade A stones just to make chips. They're made from scraps to use up all of the stone. Hopefully that helps some of you better understand the market. Also rocks n stones are very heavy. Go grab a few n weigh them. A pound of rocks is NOT much. Get your perception of rocks n chips in line with reality then you won't be disappointed n understand you are dealing with gem stones, semi precious gems crystals n choice rocks which cost a lot more than ally rocks just raw let alone the expense n time that goes into to processing them and learn how it's done also and you'll appreciate what you get and what is what a whole lot more not to mention how rare and mined out they are from mankind raping the planet of them all. Be hankful grateful 🙏 for what you get and know what your doing so you can enjoy it. My stones n rocks 101 for dummies to hopefully help some of you out and help you appreciate things in life more. Hope it helps some of you better when purchasing n understand knowledge is power n ignorance is just that, ignorance. So learn n do your homework then you won't be disappointed so much.

Thank you so much, full 5 stars all the way around and even love having the mix of base metal wires to mix n play with also. Love them to I just have allergies to anything but sterling. Great for gifts though and fun to work with anyway. Very pleased n happy with my purchase. Will recommend and would purchase again. 😊 Thank you again.

top_rant.text:
I do not want anybody to accuse me of being 'non objective' so I will admit that as a pro screen printer of many, many years, and an equipment designer and builder, I have as they say "a horse in this race". BUT I have ALWAYS said that if and when I see an SP set up that honors the reality of SP(which even hobby equipment should rise to at least a certain level of)then I will be happy to support it. I have many times referred people to various forms of supplies and equipment that are NOT of my make and that make nothing from. So...I AM quite willing and able to be objective. NOWHERE here will or do I even plug my company(do not ask me to)...I am just posing some things for you to consider based on one who has long and true experience in SP and had used every kind of equipment out there. Yes....I have.

The Yudu machine almost makes me mad. I will try to stay merely frustrated instead, so I can cover SOME of my points.
I never designed my screen printing machine 'to sell'. I had a very good idea and that idea began to raise interest in my equipment. I started out with no hi-hype mass marketing(or of poor ideas)and still do not utilize such to this day. I have videos on youtube that show my equipment and the logic of it in action and THAT (not marketing hype)will interest people or not. I stay VERY busy and i am proud to build what I build because I know that it is based on REAL WORLD experience and so I KNOW I am approaching people whgo want to get into SP with the respect of making sure they know what they will need to know and have the tools they will need to have. I make it a point to separate myself from the mere 'marketeers of' screen printing systems...by staying real to the PROCESS instead of 'PROFIT MODELS'. I'd rather you NOT buy a press from me or anybody if you do not know some basic and very important things re screen printing; even at the entry level.

Screen printing begins BEFORE you even pick up a can of ink or a squeegee or a screen or a tee. Like most things, the quality you can expect depends on the tools that are used in the process. Do you have paper plates and cups and plastic knives and forks in your cupboards? Would you want to plan or eat a carefully prepared meal on such? I would guess your answer is 'no'. Paper plates and plastic ware is fine for camping and the hotdogs and pork and beans...but you KNOW you cannot depend on it for much beyond. I want you to think of the screen printing process that same way. If you are expecting quality as an end result...then look at the tools you are investing in. They are intrinsically an inescapably connected.

Too many people approach SP with what I call a 'Disney' attitude. There are people that package and market screen printing 'set-ups' to appeal to THAT mentality and NOT anything that serves the reality of the trade. I love screen printing. I would be ashamed to offer this kind of equipment to anybody. Even a beginner. Many have bought it and will continue to buy it...that is the nature of natural ignorance, and what the business models of too many companies is centered on. Don't believe me?

Question: YUDU includes and promotes the use of water based ink. The WORST ink for new printers to have to use or learn with. BUT...the bag that comes with the YUDU has a print on it that is NOT water based ink! It is plastisol(heat cure).(Yes YUDU!...we who know the difference CAN spot that!) Hmmmm. I guess the printers that print the bags for them(you did not actually think that bag was printed using a YUDU, did you?)KNOW that heat cure ink is the best to use, especially for multiple item printing. The YUDU kit and process just does not want to deal with a few extra things regarding that reality and it may look good up front, but it does not best serve the reality of screen printing. Call up nearly any pro screen printer and ask them what they think of water based inks for beginner printers. Water based inks belong in art and craft stores...to sell to those who do not know or care to learn the benefits of heat cure. There are some(very few)higher end/formulations of water base ink but it is NOT what is in the YUDU box. It is HIGHLY amusing to me that YUDU proves what I just said by the print sample(the bag)that comes WITH THEIR OWN KIT!

Pick up the plastic piece of nonsense that YUDU passes off as an ink spreader/squeegee and then look at a REAL squeegee that screen printers actually use. YUDU's version will be found in NO screen print shop anywhere. You need the right tool to do the job right. Look at the type of ink(water base is the hardest to use and is the worst choice especially for learning SP)YUDU supplies/sells and then compare the cost/quantity factor to what you can get from a REAL screen printing ink supplier. The profit motive will then already be clear to you by doing just that. However, it goes far beyond that. YUDU has accomplished the irony of creating a screen printing kit that will have you working harder and spending more than you need to. and yet the marketing is such that YOU(who do not know any better)will THINK you are getting a handy and convenient way to screen print. I have from 3 to 7 or so prospective customers a week asking me my opinion on the YUDU. The strange thing is, that there is SO much that is not right about it...I almost feel it would be easier to just say nothing...let out a big sigh...and then move onto the next email. The videos I already have up should have made it clear to them by a few simple deductions.

BUT...I care about the reality of the process and how people begin. SP has been a very popular industry for so many years. EVERYBODY has an idea for a tee shirt and THAT, again, serves as ALL the reason companies that do not know or truly care about screen printing reality, can and are only to happy to sell you a marketing model more than a real entrance into a real and time honored process. You can bet that in the back room of YUDU that profit and marketing models and graphs are being defined/refined and studied. What you do NOT see...is the result of all that ending up as a REAL squeegee, or BETTER ink to benefit YOU. YET, the bottom line of SP is that when it is just YOU and the inks/screens/squeegees etc, you will either know and be comfortable with what you are doing or you will not. I wish I could spend ONE HOUR with those who praise the YUDU. I would very quickly7 and easily show them/you what YUDU is not telling you to make your SP life easier and WAY cheaper.

I have to elaborate on that. How many of us have bought a cheap printer and then get nailed on replacement cartridges? Yeah...it's like that. For example: a REAL and correctly made/tensioned screen printing frame costs LESS than what YUDU passes off as a screen printing frame/screen. Marketeers KNOW that the real money to be made is not so much on the front end(thought IMO the YUDU is overpriced)but when you go to buy supplies. I went to craft world last night and wrote down what the inks and accessories cost and all the while I was thinking about how much less i pay for far better things at REAL screen print suppliers! Yeah...it miffed me. I am a real screen printer and THAT is how I approach teaching others or selling them equipment. I get plenty of emails from people who watch my vids NOT BUYING ANYTHING FROM ME and just thanking me for raising there awareness and THAT makes me as happy inside as when I sell a press! BECAUSE my first goal was and is and always will be to enlighten people regarding a trade I love and KNOW intensely well. Because I know that tools and training are JUST like a good roadmap. It is the ONLY way to get where you want to go in a way that can satisfy. I don't sell supplies nor ridiculously mark them up. I send my customers to the same true wholesalers (screens/squeegees/inks)I use, so they can get the same deals I get. when you see a company doing ANYTHING ELSE, then the profit motive has likely taken over. We are NOT the same.

I am here, juxtaposed amongst all this 'marketing' of so-called screen printing kits and I find it very odd that in spite of all my REAL and EARNED skills and knowledge of screen printing...I come off as the eternal underdog because 'they' put up marketing campaigns that are so exhaustive that most people will never know of me...or more importantly: how much of a better and more reality based start(and so right growth)they could have had in Screen printing. I get accused of being too passionate about the realities of this industry and I have never nor w2ill ever apologize for that. I get accused of "rambling". Then don't listen or read it. I will happily let anybody who is convinced my either marketing or ignorance, that YUDU and so many like them, is the easiest and best...to let you get back to that. I KNOW very few will hear me, next to those who will fall into the "you can be a screen printer!' marketing nets.

I "ramble"...my passion is from YEARS of real experience...good times and very hard. And yet some cannot even listen to a 10 minute video or read about realities without being offended that I actually have something to say and a reality base to say it from. If you are an impatient person...you will not want to get into screen printing PERIOD. Much less fall into a marketing scheme. At the risk of "rambling" about realities, I choose THAT instead of hype that leaves you actually nowhere soon enough. These same people who accuse me of rambling will sit through hours of mindless TV and commercials and not bat an eye. I work hard at SP and I speak the truth about what I know. So...yeah...it is a kind of offense to me that I even have to say anything to warn anybody about these things.

I can tell you that easily 90 percent of people who get into screen printing fall out of it quickly. Thgis is almost always due to lack of truth and understanding during the equipment purchase process, and the realities of the actual procedures. Real screen printing is NOT like the 'Disney' thinking that sells so much equipment. YES...I have a YUDU. You know how I got it??? I traded a customer one of MY presses for it. He bought a YUDU; realized he "made a mistake" (HIS WORDS)and honsetly admitted that to me when inquiring about the gear I offer. MOST just silently chalk up their mistakes to 'lesson learned'. ONLY BECAUSE I get so many asking me about YUDU, did I get the idea to trade him to get one. You will soon see my reviews of it on youtube. Please do not ask me to trade me your YUDU...for my equipment...I only did it this ONE time so I can address better(in video)what needs to be addressed. Take it or leave it. If you are happy with a/your YUDU then more power to you. My question is: How much did you know about SP realities BEFORE you invested in it? If you knew what I and many real screen printers know about ink types and tool types; and the overall process...I think you would be better off to have known. 'DU YU' know? I DU!

NONE of this is to 'get you to buy' from ME. I would rather people not get into SP AT ALL...than to follow a way that will make it harder and more expensive than it needs to be. I could have thrown anything in a box and called it a screen printing kit. I CHOSE to make sure that people EVEN AT THE START know about and get the things that are worthy of learning and growing with. I did not get into screen printing because i am lazy or stupid. it is NOT as easy as it LOOKS and yet that apparent ease sure sells a LOT of gear for some marketers. I repeat: I LOVE my trade. But if I did not know it as I do I would not be offering equipment to anybody. What comes out of the YUDU box and the processes/tools/supplies , lets me know that YUDU is thinking about something ELSE. I gave them one star for being great marketing magicians.

I will finish up two presses today...and be VERY proud that whether the customers are among the few who actually stay and or grow with SP...I did my job RIGHT. Whether they ever contact me or not...that I am HERE with the REAL skills and knowledge to assist. Equipment is just the first step. Training is everything. When I see companies profiting at your expense(not just what they want up front)...it bothers me. Good starts in screen printing are not marketing campaigns and they cannot be bought. So when I see that the box does not even have the right tools to honor the process(not just 'accomplish' it)it bugs me. THAT is not the best side of screen printing. I know it and I strive to help others know it. I have no issue regarding 'budget' or lower cost screen printing kits. I serve that industry myself. BUT...I just feel that the bar should be higher than YUDU places it. There is a lack of attention to reality in the YUDU box that just cannot be ignored. Notice how they do NOT show any shirt after shirt after shirt runs on the videos. There is a reason for that. it is nearly impossible with water based ink, to do longer runs without experiencing some major challenges due to the ink(water based)they choose to provide(instead of heat cure). NO new printer wil;l be able to have the skill or speed to keep ahead of those issues. Especially while trying to load a tee on what they call a 'platen'. YUDU has designed this equipment agsinst logic by marrying processes tools and supplies that work against quality and the realities of SP more than one or a few shirts at a time. I will prove it(all to easily and with NO marketing ploys or sleight of hand)in my video review of.

Especially as an experienced screen printer(so it would be easier for ME to use this equipment than the non experienced...obviously)..I would not waste my time(process needlessly long and too many steps), or money(too many items and IMO overpriced)to use it. So 'R YU' sure YOU want a 'YUDU'?

After I make my reviews of it...I really do not know WHAT I will do with it!? I do not want YOU to possibly end up thinking and feeling the same way. I have had several people who bought YUDU spend more to get my equipment. I HATE to see people have to(not that they had to but they DID!)double spend...because they did not know enough up front.

Don't buy ANYTHING!...until you investigate further. I hope I just helped you. If you thought it was "rambling"...then don't get into SP! The smallest job will be harder to do that the effort it took for you to read this. As long as i can help people with REAL SP info...'A rambling I will go'! All the while I know people are going to do what they will do. THAT is why I do what I do. I had the chance as a real screen printer to take the higher road, and I took it. I had to decide between mass profits or truth in the process and I chose and choose to represent the truth. If these things I say were/are not true then it would not be so easy for me to prove each, and more. This is for those who WANT to know. Not everybody does!

I would love to tell you something better about the YUDU. But I cannot. This 'thing' IMO is an insult to an industry I love and YOU deserve better for your motivation money and trust. I met a lady who had all the motivation in the world to SP. She bought into a budget system that did not offer the tools or teach her what she needed to know(LOT of that around!)and she was SO disappointed by the experience that she refused to even let me show her how things are done right. I was oddly embarassed that somebody else had misrepresented my trade to her and at her expense. I wanted her to at least see that SP (and real sp'ers)are not what she encountered. Too late...she moved into(and had a very good home business with!...that might have been SP has she started with the right stuff and knowledge).

I cannot say whether that was a YUDU or not? But I have seen it happen with others and it was after they bought a YUDU. Fortunately, at least in a few cases, that did not stop them. I am happy they chose me on 'second try'...but it does bother me that they got mislead at all...to begin with. As they say: "that is gonna leave a mark!". I want to see people get a GOOD start. The foundation is of immense importance to the progress made. ONLY a real screen printer knows that. The 'marketeers' do not get that...but I hope YOU will.

HAPPY SCREEN PRINTING! ( I hope)
top_rant.title: JUST SAY NO...TO YUDU.

top_rant.text:
*Note* - At the time I reviewed this product, the price was around $12. It's now dropped to almost half that, which actually makes it a reasonable unit for the price. For this price, I feel it's worth it, especially if you want to modify it a little to make up for some of its shortcomings.

Now, lower the lights, and roll film!
-------------------------------------

... For those who don't already have a big head. Scratch that, this won't boost your ego sufficiently... or maybe at all...

It's cheap, it's light, it works, it shipped really quickly... but it's definitely not the Rolls Royce of head mounted magnifiers. First off, it definitely feels like ~$12 strapped to your head. No... I have to scratch that too. My cheapie black baseball caps from eBay cost 1/3 the price and are FAR more comfortable. This definitely feels like a sharp plastic band cutting into your forehead. Also, the magnifier front portion that is attached to the headband, flops forward no matter how you force the seemingly oversized and overly strong aluminum bolts to tighten it. I used it twice on the first night I got it, and I finally just gave up and let it flop because my forehead stopped it at just the right angle. More on that in a bit. ;-)

The lenses work well. It indeed has 3 levels of magnification, and if you use the highest, you're working with something VERY tiny indeed. There is a considerable amount of edge distortion, and I found it a little tricky to keep my head still so that the image was clear enough when working with the really tiny stuff, but it's a practice thing and I simply haven't used one of these long enough. What do you want for ~$12? BTW, I may use that phrase A LOT for this review.

The LED light does in fact work, and it does in fact come off very bluish. It's tolerable, but it would be pretty slick if replaced with a brighter and whiter LED. :-)

The first time I used it, I did some soldering of fine wires, with lap joints to connect them tip to tip. It did well, but it was a simple job and it wasn't really pushing the limits of "tiny". The second use was more pressing - I discovered that my finger was sore in one spot because somewhere in my workday I picked up a sliver. It was a tiny one, so I had to use magnification level 2, and I needed very bright light, and a sewing needle to pick the sliver out. The procedure took a lot longer than I expected it to, and I did have a very hard time keeping my head in alignment with my finger so that I wasn't drifting the view to one side or another. Just a little to one side, and things got really blurred, and likewise with too far or too near - a lot of blurring if I wasn't holding my head JUST RIGHT.

I was at it so long with that stupid sliver (which turned out to be a chip of porcelain) that I became sort of immersed in the world of the head magnifier, and by the time I got the little bastard out of my finger, I had lost all concept of how tiny it really was. That was a big nod to the head magnifier - it worked well enough to give me pause when I lifted the magnifier away and saw how very tiny the sliver actually was.

Then too, I also had a big moment of pause and a big laugh when I looked at myself in the bathroom mirror and saw the huge red crease across my forehead - the harsh plastic edges had been cutting in for quite a while and I'd desensed to it a bit. It's time to say it again - What do you want for ~$12?

Long and short - they work. They work well for how little you spend to get into basic head mounted magnifiers. The selection of magnifications is nice, but there are a LOT of areas for improvement. One, the sloppy "can't tighten this friggin thing" front part is really annoying. Two, those sharp edges, good God, really! And three, why the blue LED? Blue light is hard for human eyes to focus on and the worst in the world for such an application. Fix these three major pains in the a**, and you've got something really good going here.

All that said, what do you want for ~$12? ;-)

A little update, 7-13-13: It's been quite a while since I bought this thing, and I'm surprising myself to say that it's kind of become indispensable. I do a lot of work with soldering, and as it happens now I feel awkward as h*** doing any soldering WITHOUT it, because I can see my work area just so much better, magnified. Additionally, I occasionally fabricate my own printed circuit board designs, and I've found that for drilling out the PCB pads, it's become as indispensable as with my soldering. Simply put, I can nail all the holes dead center with ease, where without any visual aid I tend to hit a bit off center. Makes ALL the difference. It's especially valuable when looking for tiny solder bridges, cracks in traces or other such micro-fine trouble spots in things. I also find a lot of use with it in reading part numbers on chips, because sometimes the print can be so faded-looking no matter what the light angle. Frankly, I've gotten to the point where I don't hardly care anymore how floppy and uncomfortable it can be. I just do the work, and completely ignore any irritation on my forehead. That said, as before, I am not using it for an 8+ hour a day job. Were that the case, I'd definitely want to either improve or replace it. For hobby uses, it's fast becoming my BFF for a LOT of the things I do.

Another update, 8-17-13: I finally got off my *** and did the LED upgrade to the illuminator unit for this head mounted magnifier. It was surprisingly easy, owing to the fact that the illuminator unit comes out of the magnifier very easily (just rotate up and pull out!) and disassembles just as easily (four screws and no locks). The LEDs themselves were just common 5mm/T1-3/4 LEDs, and are soldered into a tiny piece of PCB in parallel with each other. They fit into a plastic reflector assembly, and were a little hard to get out of it but I managed to pry them out with the tip of an X-Acto knife without any damage, rocking back and forth between the two LEDs. I then removed the two power wires to make the rest of the work easier on me. Desoldering them was pretty much a piece of cake, using a Radio Shack vacuum 45W desoldering IRON (not a common solder sucker) although one of the solder pads showed signs of peeling after the LED removal was done so be careful about board damage. Prying again with the X-Acto, I was able to remove both LEDs easily enough, and then I replaced them with a pair of 27,000 MCD 5mm white LEDs from [...] (eBay). They soldered in easily, I resoldered the tiny wires (make sure you get em right!!) and then the whole thing went back together in seconds. I did all this work, mind you, with the magnifier on my head but obviously without the illuminator. I like this thing more every day, lol. Anyway... End results! It's definitely less blue, more comfortable to look at, but there still seems to be a bit of a bluish cast that is probably coming from that cheap, metallized plastic reflector. Not sure what to say about that, but I'm sure it could be worked on. It could also be coming from the plastic lens that sits over the LEDs, which could be removed easily enough I'm sure. Also, the LEDs I used are probably brighter but the difference is so little that I couldn't really be sure there was a gain at all. Anyway, it's good enough, the work was easy, and had I been in a racing mood I'm sure I could have done it in less than 5 minutes instead of the ten or so it did take me. I'm pleased enough - it's an upgrade. Now I just want to work out some kind of padding to make the unit more comfy, and I'll be plenty happy with it. :-)

Rick NR417

WARNING: NOT YELLING IN ALL CAPS, JUST LEGALLY BLIND.
I AM A BEGINNER IN WATERCOLOR AND FOUND TUBE PAINTS INTIMIDATING. I HAD PURCHASED A WATERCOLOR PENCIL SET FROM ARTEZA AND FOUND IT WANTING IN SO MANY AREAS, PIGMENT VIBRANCY, LED QUALITY, SHARPENABILTY AND I FOUND THEIR CUSTOMER SERVICE UNREACHABLE, JUST TO NAME A FEW ISSUES I HAD WITH THE ARTEZA SET.

WHEN I FOUND THIS CASTLE ARTS SET I THOUGHT I WOULD GIVE IT A TRY. THAT WAS OVER A MONTH AGO, I WAS AND STILL AM SIMPLY AMAZED BY THIS GREAT SET!
THE PROS:
*THE SET HAS A WIDE VARIETY OF HIGHLY VIBRANT PIGMENTED COLORS!
*THEY WORK VERY WELL IN LAYERING COLORS DRY ON DRY TO CREATE A WET WASH OR EVEN WET ON WET TO CREATE DIFFERANT COLORS AND SHADES.
*USING THEM IN WET ON WET, I'VE FOUND BY DIPPING THE PENCIL LED INTO CLEAN WATER FOR 4-5 SECONDS THE COLOR EXPLODES IN A DAZZLING WAY ON PAPER!
*THE PENCILS ARE MADE OF QUALITY LED & WOOD MAKING THEM VERY EASY TO SHARPEN AND I HAVE YET TO HAVE A SINGLE LED BREAK!!! (TIP: WHEN SHARPENING WATERCOLOR PENCILS USE A HAND SHARPENER AND ALWAYS TURN THE SHARPENER NEVER THE PENCIL.)
*THEY VERY WORK WELL, DRY ON DRY, WET ON DRY AND WET ON WET!

*THE CASE IS VERY STURDY MAKING IT EASY TO STORE AND TRANSPORT. I LOVE THIS CASE!
*IT IS VERY EASY TO TAKE PIGMANT DIRECTLY FROM THE LED WITH A WET BRUSH TO EITHER PAINT OR CREATE A MIXING OF VIBRANT COLORS ON A PALLET!
*THEY ARE WORTH EVERY PENNY!
*THEY WOULD BE A GREAT GIFT FOR BUDDING ARTISTS AND A NICE SET TO GIFT FOR PROFESSIONAL ARTISTS.
*CASTLE ARTS CUSTOMER SERVICE IS HANDS DOWN, IMPRESSIVE AND OUTSTANDING!

CONS:
* THEIR DEMO VIDEOS AND SUPPLIMENTY PDF INSTRUCTIONS ARE SERIOUSLY LACKING.
*THE COLOR PRINT ON THE WATERCOLOR PENCILS ARE INCREDIBLY SMALL COMPARED TO OTHERS ON THE MARKET!!! I SPEND AN INORDIANT AMOUNT OF TIME TRYING TO FIND THE COLORS I WANT IN MY PAINTINGS. YOU CAN SEE THE COMPARRISON DIFFERENCE IN PIC #4 CASTLE ARTS IS ON THE TOP.
*I WISH THEY HAD COME ORGANIZED BY COLOR FAMILY INSTEAD OF NUMERICAL ORDER. I'VE SPENT CONSIDERABLE HOURS RE-ARRANGING THEM INTO A EASILY USABLE FASHION.

top_screaming.text:
THIS CLAY IS A WONDERFUL PRODUCT EVEN FOR BEGINNERS. YOU CAN'T MESS UP AND WASTE IT NO MATTER WHAT YOU DO. BECAUSE EVEN AFTER IT HAS DRIED IT CAN BE RESTORED TO USABLE PRODUCT AGAIN WITH MINIMAL LOSS SIMPLY BY PUTTING IT TO SOAK IN WATER. DEPENDING ON THE THICKNESS OF THE DRIED PROJECT IT MAY TAKE A WHILE BUT IT WILL MELT BACK DOWN.
TO RESTORE A PROJECT TO USABLE CLAY:
I RECOMMEND BREAKING THE PROJECT INTO SMALLER PIECES AND COVERING IT IN WARM OR HOT WATER. (I LIKE TO USE AN OLD MARGARINE TUB WITH A LID) THEN ONCE IT HAS MELTED DOWN, WITHOUT DISTURBING THE CONTAINER A LOT, POUR OFF THE EXCESS WATER AND KNEED THE CLAY TOGETHER TO REMOVE ANY LUMPS AND REGAIN CONSISTENCY, FOLLOWED BY ALLOWING IT TO AIR DRY JUST UNTIL IT IS BACK TO ITS ORIGINAL FORM.
ON A SIDE NOTE** THE KID IN ME LOVES THIS BECAUSE IT IS LIKE BEING A CHILD AGAIN AND PLAYING IN THE MUD AND AS IT IS NON-TOXIC IT DOESN'T HARM THE SKIN!
AS FOR FINISHED PROJECTS, I HAVE FOUND IT TO BE VERY SATISFACTORY IN SCULPTING THINGS WITH AN EXCEPTION: WHEN MAKING ITEMS THAT ARE THICK AND SOLID THERE CAN BE CRACKING AS THE ITEM DRIES FROM THE OUTSIDE IN. THIS CAN BE REMEDIED USUALLY BY:
1. ALLOWING IT TO DRY SLOWLY AWAY FROM HEAT AND SUNLIGHT.
2. WATCHING IT CLOSELY FOR SIGNS OF CRACKING AND MOISTENING ANY CRACKS WITH WATER RUBBING THEM OUT AS THEY APPEAR.
3. FOR THICK PROJECTS USE SOME SORT OF FILLER FOR THE CENTER SPACE AND ADD THE CLAY AROUND THE FILLER KEEPING THE CLAY THICKNESS TO A MINIMUM EVEN WADDED NEWSPAPER CAN WORK AS FILLER, I HAVE USED STYRAFOAM AND EVEN OLD PILL BOTTLES TO BUILD ON.
4. THICK PROJECTS CAN ALSO BE BUILT IN LAYERS ALLOWING EACH LAYER TO DRY BEFORE ADDING THE NEXT, JUST DON'T FORGET TO DAMPEN THE FIRST LAYER BEFORE ADDING MORE CLAY OR IT WON'T ADHERE AND CAN COME APART AS THE NEW LAYER DRIES.
5. KEEPING THE OUTER LAYER DAMP CAN HELP WITH CRACKING I USE A DAMPENED PIECE OF CHEESECLOTH TO COVER MY PROJECTS WHILE THEY DRY SLOWLY DON'T FORGET TO REDEMPEN THE CLOTH AS NEEDED!

My surface is ruined completely unless I fix it with ONE MORE layer of epoxy!!!!!!!!!! This epoxy was getting hotter and hotter within matter of minutes!! Then finally it became lumpy and clumpy!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! I have to do tons of additional work to fix this now!! How in the world does this resin cure so fast !!!! In such a small area!!! top_five_star_obscene.text:
It's kind of hard to review feathers, feathers are feathers.

I bought these to be the hen feathers on some arrows I was fletching after removing plastic vanes. I shoot a longbow and plastic vanes don't work in a longbow where you're shooting either off your hand (traditional English style longbows) or off the arrow shelf in modern longbows and flat bows. The plastic vanes don't compress as they pass over your hand/the shelf and it kicks the arrow tail-out from the side of the bow you're shooting from. It flies off-axis and will always impact the target with the nock badly offside from the point. This means the arrow is both inaccurate and slows down much quicker than it should.

So I got some cheap arrows that were spined correctly but had plastic vanes and I wanted to fletch them with feathers for practice arrows, saving my good arrows for hunting.

Which brings us to these feathers. They are very uniformly cut, they have a good base of feather core for gluing, they are nice and straight and can be using in a fletching jig for either straight or right hand helical mounting. I have both a straight and a helical clamp but I use the straight clamp and adjust the jig to give me a very slight right hand spin using the straight clamp. I find this works well in my bow because it spins the arrow enough to stabilize it well without whirling it like a high speed gyroscope which increases air resistance and slows down the arrow.

I used a different brand and color for cock feathers and they were indistinguishable from these as far as quality, shape and uniformity is concerned. They were more expensive though, so next time I'll buy these and stripe the cock feather with a permanent marker to aid in correct nocking position.

The price on these was as good and mostly better than anything else I could find on Amazon and they are all highly uniform and perfectly cut.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Good price on good feathers.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I have the yarka St Petersburg "sequel" set. I love them. Love the intensityixability and lay down of color.

These sonnet watercolors have got to be made in the same factory... Some pigment profiles, pour patterns, even the pans are identical.

it looks like this watercolor set has almost all the same colors that are in the Yorker original set of 24. It's a good compliment to the sequel set and the expanded set if you were looking to supplement your existing Yarka watercolors.

you can basically buy this exact same madine empty watercolor tin with the insert for about $18 empty. So for $11 more you also get a set of watercolors that on average costs $50 to $80 from essentially a different packaging scheme. I would not be surprised if the Leningrad paints that are here on Amazon are also the exact same paints as yarka st. Petersburg and as Jack Richeson White nights. It's definitely a good purchase. It's a fantastic starters kit as well.

I have tried a pretty extensive number of watercolors and manufacturers at this point. I have a good number of Daniel Smith paints and I really like those unique and earthy pigments and as well as the extensive profiles. I do like the way M. Graham watercolors work onto the page and mix, and they're a good value for the price however something about them leaves me a little bit flat and I don't like doing an entire painting with them. I really do like the yarka pans and these are essentially the same paints. I also have the Mijello gold class set of 36 paints which is another fantastic set to start off with if that's all you're going to purchase for watercolor. I also have a few tubes of Sennelelier, Win-ton artist grade, grumbacher, as well as Derwent and Faber castell and arteza watercolor pencils.

Overall if you have a $30 budget I'd say by this and you can really get a heck of a start with some really nice paints. They're full pans so you get quite a bit of paint.

If you buy a few tubes here and thereor perhaps a few supplemental pans if there's a color you really really want to get a new pigment worked into your set this is really the way to go.

If you are new to watercoloring I would really advise against buying some crappy off brand manufactured pan sets. They're going to be terrible and you're not going to enjoy the learning process anywhere near as well as you will working with the real thing. I would really avoid any of the u.s. art supply or strange names that don't have any recognition 'sets' of things. once in awhile you might stumble across something good but by the time you buy three or four sets of crappy color pigments you may as well have just bought something halfway decent to begin with and enjoyed working with them the whole time.

If I had a terribly low-budget I would say get this set, some Princeton student grade brushes in a package with various brush size and shape options of synthetic but decent quality bristles, and a canson XL watercolor pad of whatever size you like, maybe start with a 9 by 12. You might also try some Strathmore 400 series watercolor paper. You could probably get all of those things for around $55-$65 and be happily on your way.

But basically you really can't go wrong with this particular set because you get a watercolor box worth 15 to $20 and you get 24 watercolors that if you bought them under another brand name would cost you upwards of fifty bucks.

Go for it.

My set of Yarkas are on the left. The Sonett colors included with this paintbox are on the right. If you're looking at the swatch grids that I painted anything that's a yarka pigment has a y in the number and anything that's a sonnet has an s with the number. The ones pictured in the white pallette box are the combination of the Yonkers and the sonnetts that I put together since the box holds 36 that came with my Yarka set. I'm using the leftover paints and the box that came with this set to include some half pans of colors that I have in tubes with another set.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Yarka?  You betcha.  Worth it?  Yup!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I'm just getting to the point where single-vision glasses aren't really doing it, and I have progressive lenses now to make up the difference. However, I really can't see the up-close detail I used to take for granted.

However, I'm not ready to just give up and wear readers on a chain around my neck or get one of those lighted plastic magnifiers you see the older generations using to read the paper and whatnot. I mostly need magnification for "cool" (to me) stuff like examining circuit boards and so on, so I wanted something cool to do it with. Not some plastic monstrosity.

My biggest fear ordering this is that it would be some cheap piece of crap that looks good in pictures but not in person: good from afar, but far from good. This was not the case.

The magnifying glass is hefty. It has a really good weight in the hand, balanced towards the lens quite a bit but not so much that the handle feels light and cheap. I'm pretty sure it's not aluminum colored to look like brass - not sure what it is, but it feels too heavy to be aluminum. I'm sure at this price the materials aren't premium, but the lens is definitely glass, it's very clear, and the whole thing feels solid and well-made.

The result is a magnifying glass that doesn't make me feel like an old man to use, and I'm not embarrassed to hand it to someone else if we're looking at tiny stuff - they're not going to pick it up and think I have some cheap crappy magnifier.

And if you're shopping around let me give you a little advice: I bought this one and a 10x magnifying glass that's similar in appearance. If you're going to buy just one, get this one. 5x is plenty of magnification for reading very tiny print or just about anything else. I'm happy to have the 10x as well, but this one will be much more useful.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Great weight in hand, Looks beautiful

top_five_star_obscene.text:
There are generic cheap knitting needle tip protectors you can buy by the dozens. I have. The plastic is harder, stiffer and much less grippy than Clover's gummy-textured rubber tips.

Live and learn from my cheapness, knitter friends. Clovers don't age, dry out or let go when you really don't want a bunch of stitches escaping your needles in the project bag--like 1000 stitches of a circular lace shawl that went walkabout on me due to crappy cheap tip protectors giving up the ghost in my bag.

Cue the "Psycho" violins and blood-curdling scream.

I feel the same way about Clover stitch markers, made of the same resilient, non-crappy rubber as the tip protectors. Clovers stay in place, slide fine and don't fling themselves off the needle at the very point you're about to slip them and knit onward. I have chased too many cheap rubber markers to consider them a good bargain. I wish I could buy more Clovers in a package, but whatever. If you want to save on Clover, go to a store. I love the convenience of Prime and the quality of Clover.

top_five_star_obscene.text: okaaaaaaayyyyyyyyy... it's supposed to be water proof... right !!.... short story. I purchased new floor matts for an old car. I decided to treat them up front using the 303 product. I Treated on a Thursday night - dried overnight - put matts in car. Fast forward to Monday afternoon. I drove from New Orleans to Houston and I kept being bothered by a few slivers of silver trash on my new grey matts. I kept thinking. "my passenger must have dropped trash on the floor on Sunday.. no one had sat over there today(monday) "...... I completed my journey to Houston and immediately opened the passenger door to remove the matt and dump the silver trash and other minimal debris. when I picked up the matt, the silver trash "rolled" accross the matt surface......... It was actually several beads of water, some of which were in 2 inch long strings. It had sat on the treated surface ot the matt for two days including a 6 hour ride with motion and vibration. Another strange but complimentary note, the spill was actually on an inclined portion of the matt..... I dont know how long it will last, but this stuff is certainly starting out with 5 stars...  the silver appearance was related to the water drops refracting the grey carpet.. the drops looked silver, so I thought it was silver trash.....

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Well I hope I become the most magnificent colored pencil artist of all time!!! I am drowning in fantastic quality colored pencils. I already have the 72 set of Prismacolors but saw the amazing reviews for these and for the price I couldn't pass them up. So I ordered the 180 set. Then I saw a video reviee by a colored pencil artist who said the 180 pack doesn't have the same high quality as the pencils in the 120 pack. She said it was barely noticeable to her. But leave it to me to snatch up yet another set, the 120 set. I also have a bunch of cheap cheap crappy colored pencils and another set that's in the mail from Germany. Ugh. I am just trying to figure out what makes me think I need all of these. Anyway, they're really great. As you can see I used their swatching paper just because it was already printed, I don't have a printer and let's face it, I haven't even swatched my Prismacolor set or half of my markers yet so I have a LOT of swatching to do!

These hold a very sharp point without breaking and lay down buttery thick pigment. They're truly a dream. In many ways they seem better than Prismacolors. I got a cracked pencil all the way top to bottom in my 72 set of Prismacolors and the sharpener I bought that was sealed in their packaging had a giant piece of broken graphite tip in the bottom of the smaller hole side and then the top broke so I can't empty it. I had to order a new one. Thankfully I got a good electric one or my hands would be so pissed at me.
These pencils don't have crooked cores like Prismacolor so far as I have been sharpening them. The bottom end is covered so you can't see the "leads". So far so good. I have to say again. I was shocked when I got a really sharp long point and expected the tip to break right away so I went in softly but there weren't even the smallest crumbs so I pressed harder and harder and it stayed absolutely perfect.
In my photos the 120 set has 3 tiers of pencils and they come in a good color order also unlike Prismacolor which I had to group into like colors since they were all randomly placed.
They do come "sharpened" but very slightly. You'll have to sharpen them as you use them (unless you want to do them all at once). They come in a metal tin with a hinged lid that's really nice because it gives you a way to pull a tier out and put it in the lid that you can lean against something and have two tiers right in front of you and then you can just put a tier on the table next to the tin. The pencils are square and are numbered WITH color names in English on them. That didn't carry over to the 180 set. Those pencils are black and round and have a smaller barrel and don't have color names. So I plan to have some fun naming them!!
So in my pictures you can see the 120 set each pencil has the color down the entire barrel and the 180 set does not, they're all black with a pop of color on the back end. The 180 tin is no longer hinged and the lid just lifts off. There are 6 tiers of pencils in this tin. The 120 tiers have elastic bands to pull them out on both sides. They didn't do that with the 180 set. I might just need to eat a LOT of large tubs of yogurt as they seem to be the perfect size and shape to store a large amount of colored pencils. I want to keep the colors together.
These are fine for beginners. Kids as well, they're sturdy.
So that's my review and comparison of the two sets and how they stand up to Prismacolor.
top_five_star_obscene.title: I compared the 120 set to 180 set and how they stand up to Prismacolors
